1: wᵉyithyaḥasû — the verb yāḥas (to be enrolled/registered by genealogy) appears in the Hithpael reflexive here: wayyithyaḥasû kol yiśrāʾēl, ‘and all Israel enrolled themselves.’ The Hithpael signals community self-identification — the act of registering in the genealogies is active, not merely assigned. sēper malkê yiśrāʾēl — ‘the book of the kings of Israel’ is the Chronicler’s most frequently cited source (appearing with variations throughout 1–2 Chronicles); it is distinct from canonical Kings. bᵉmaʿalām — ‘because of their unfaithfulness’ (Qal infinitive construct of māʿal); this word will recur as the Chronicler’s verdict on Saul in 1 Chronicles 10:13 — the exile of Judah and the death of Saul are both attributed to the same word-root, binding national history to royal failure under one interpretive category.
2: nᵉtînîm — ‘temple servants,’ literally ‘the given ones’ (Qal passive participle of nātan, to give). The form encodes the category: persons given over to temple service, likely descendants of captives assigned as support staff for the Levites (Numbers 31:47; Joshua 9:27; Ezra 2:43-54). The term occurs 17× in Ezra-Nehemiah and 2× in Chronicles, marking a service class whose origins predate the exile but whose community identity is reconstituted in the post-exilic settlement. The listing of Israelites, priests, Levites, and Nethinim as four distinct groups reflects the Chronicler’s consistently stratified view of the worshipping community.
3: The enumeration of settlers from Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh in Jerusalem is theologically significant: Chronicles includes northern-tribe representation in the restored Jerusalem community. Samuel-Kings largely writes off the north after the 722 BCE Assyrian deportation; the Chronicler envisions a reunified Israel around the Davidic-Levitical center. The grammatical structure is a simple listing with wayyēšᵉḇû (waw-consecutive Qal imperfect of yāšaḇ, ‘to settle/dwell’) — the narrative past tense marking the settlement as a completed, definitive act.
4: A genealogical line from Perez son of Judah through five patronymic links to Uthai — the ben-chain establishes legitimacy of settlement for the post-exilic community. The Perez lineage is the Davidic sub-clan (Ruth 4:18-22; 1 Chronicles 2:4-15), giving this first settler special prestige. No linguistic variants from the parallel in Nehemiah 11:4.
5: šîlōnî — the ‘Shilonites’ are descendants of Shelah son of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:3; Numbers 26:20), not Shiloh the city. The designation habbe̊kôr (‘the firstborn’) applied to Asaiah signals priority of standing within this clan.
6: bᵉnê zeraḥ — the Zerahite sub-clan of Judah (Zerah son of Judah, twin of Perez); Jeuel and kinsmen totaling 690. The Zerahites are the lineage of Achan/Achar (1 Chronicles 2:6-7), but here they appear as legitimate settlers — the genealogy includes even the troubled line.
7: Benjaminite genealogical line to Sallu: meshullām ben hōḏawyāh ben hassᵉnûʾāh — three patronymic links. Parallel in Nehemiah 11:7 has slight variations (mᵉšullam ben yôʿēd ben pᵉdāʾyāh ben qôlāʾyāh ben maʿaśēyāh ben ʾîṯîʾēl ben yᵉšaʿyāh), suggesting either different individuals or textual variants in the transmission. The Chronicler’s shorter list may preserve an earlier form.
8: Three more Benjaminite settlers listed with genealogical depth: Ibneiah through Jeroham; Elah through Uzzi and Michri; Meshullam through a longer chain including Shephatiah, Reuel, and Ibnijah. The variety of lineage depths (one, two, four links) reflects varying completeness in the source registers rather than editorial inconsistency.
9: ʾālpayim šᵉlōšâ wᵉḥāmišîm wᵉtišʿāh — 956 Benjaminite heads of ancestral households. The summary phrase kol ʾēlleh ʾănāšîm rāʾšê ʾāḇôt (‘all these were heads of ancestral households’) closes the genealogical unit with its standard conclusion formula, marking completeness of the register.
10: The priestly roster begins with three names — Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jachin — without full genealogical chains. Jedaiah and Jehoiarib are the two most prominent priestly families in the post-exilic period (Nehemiah 12:6; 1 Chronicles 24:7); Jehoiarib heads the first of the 24 priestly divisions (1 Chronicles 24:7). The brevity of this entry contrasts with the depth of Azariah’s genealogy in v11.
11: nāgîd bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — Azariah is identified as ‘the chief officer (nāgîd) of the house of God.’ The term nāgîd (leader, ruler, prince) is the Chronicler’s preferred title for the highest-ranking temple official — the same word used of David as YHWH’s designated king (1 Chronicles 17:7). The long genealogical chain (six links back to Zadok) establishes Azariah’s Zadokite legitimacy — the Zadokite priesthood had the exclusive claim to temple service in the Chronicler’s (and Ezekiel’s) theology (Ezekiel 40:46; 1 Chronicles 44:15).
12: Adaiah son of Jeroham with four links; Maasai son of Adiel with three links. The genealogical variety and different chain lengths likely reflects different source documents with varying levels of completeness. The Nehemiah 11:12-14 parallel preserves different versions of some of these names.
13: gibbôrê ḥayil mᵉlāʾḵat bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘capable men for the service/work of the house of God.’ The phrase gibbôrê ḥayil (men of valor/capable men) is usually military terminology (Judges 11:1; 1 Samuel 16:18); its application to priestly service here underscores that temple work is treated with the same gravity as military service. Total: 1,760 — a substantial priestly corps for the restored community.
14: The Levitical roster opens with Shemaiah son of Hasshub through to Hashabiah — assigned from the sons of Merari. The three Levitical clans (Gershon, Kohath, Merari) each provide settlers; Merari appears first here, which the Chronicler likely follows from his source registers without editorial significance.
15: Four Levites listed: Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, and Mattaniah — the last with a four-link chain through Micah and Zichri to Asaph. Asaph is one of the three great Levitical music guilds (with Heman and Jeduthun/Ethan); the Asaphite line is particularly prominent in Chronicles and in Psalm superscriptions.
16: Obadiah son of Shemaiah through Galal to Jeduthun — another guild connection (Jeduthun/Ethan is the third guild founder). Berechiah son of Asa son of Elkanah — note ʾĕlqānāh, the name also borne by Samuel’s father (1 Samuel 1:1), situating this Levite in the same tribal and vocational stream. Their residence in ḥaṣrê nᵉṭôpātî (‘the villages of the Netophathites’) reflects the Levitical settlement pattern in Judahite towns (Nehemiah 12:28).
17: šōʿᵃrîm — ‘gatekeepers,’ literally ‘those who keep the gate’ (participle of šāʿar). The four names — Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman — with their kinsmen form the core gatekeeper corps. rōʾš — Shallum is ‘the chief’ (head) of the gatekeepers; the same title will reappear in v26 for the four ‘chief gatekeepers’ (hāšōʿᵃrîm lāʾarbaʿ). The Chronicler devotes more attention to the gatekeepers than any other biblical source, reflecting their post-exilic institutional importance.
18: šaʿar hammelek qēḏmāh — ‘the king’s gate to the east.’ The Eastern Gate was the primary royal access to the temple mount; ‘formerly’ (ʿad hēnnāh, literally ‘until now’ or ‘up to this point’) signals continuity from the pre-exilic gatekeeper establishment into the post-exilic restoration — the same families, at the same gates. maḥᵃnôt bᵉnê lēwî — ‘the Levitical camps’: the wilderness camp arrangement (Numbers 2:17; 1 Chronicles 3:21-38) is invoked to establish the Levitical gatekeeping tradition as Mosaic in origin.
19: šōmᵉrê sippôt hāʾōhel — ‘guardians of the thresholds of the tent.’ The word sap/sippāh (threshold, doorstep) is used of the temple threshold with particular solemnity (Ezekiel 9:3; 1 Chronicles 10:4); it is the boundary between the holy and the profane. The Korahites are specifically identified: Shallum son of Kore son of Ebiasaph son of Korah — the same lineage as Psalms 84:10 (‘I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness’), where the Korahite authorship of the Psalms of Korah is itself a statement of Korahite identity as threshold-servants. The phrase kaʾăḇôtêhem (‘as their fathers had been’) is the key word: gatekeeping is inherited priestly vocation, not appointment.
20: pînᵉḥās ben ʾelʿāzār nāgîd ʿălêhem lᵉpānîm — ‘Phinehas son of Eleazar was formerly their chief/leader’ (nāgîd, the same term used for temple leaders in v11). The additional phrase YHWH ʿimmô — ‘and YHWH was with him’ — is the highest approbation formula in the Deuteronomistic and Chronicler’s vocabulary (used of Joshua, David, and Solomon with the same language); it validates the entire Korahite gatekeeper tradition by anchoring it to the most celebrated priestly act in Israelite history (Phinehas’s zeal at Peor, Numbers 25:7-13; Psalms 106:30-31).
21: Zechariah son of Meshelemiah at the entrance of the ʾōhel môʿēd — ‘the tent of meeting,’ the technical term for the wilderness tabernacle. The use of this archaic designation (rather than ‘house of God’) situates the post-exilic gatekeeper function within the continuous trajectory from wilderness to temple — a characteristic Chronicler rhetorical move. This Zechariah will appear again as one of the lead gatekeepers in 1 Chronicles 26:2,14.
22: mithyaḥᵃsîm — ‘enrolled in their genealogical records,’ the same Hithpael of yāḥas as v1 — the chapter is framed by the verb of genealogical self-enrollment. Total: 212. dāwid wîšᵉmûʾēl hārōʾeh — ‘David and Samuel the rōʾeh (seer) established them.’ The title rōʾeh (seer, from rāʾāh, to see) is the archaic term for a prophet (1 Samuel 9:9 explicitly glosses it: ‘the one now called a prophet was formerly called a seer’). The Chronicler designates Samuel as hārōʾeh — not hannāḇîʾ — preserving the older title; Samuel’s own books use both. The claim that David and Samuel jointly ‘established’ (yissᵉdûm, Piel of yāsaḏ, to found/establish) the gatekeeping posts roots the institution in the two figures who stand at the hinge of Israelite history.
23: šaʿarê bêt YHWH lᵉbêt hāʾōhel — ‘the gates of the house of YHWH — the house of the tent.’ The phrase apposits the Solomonic temple (‘house of YHWH’) with the wilderness tabernacle (‘the tent’) — the Chronicler’s standard collapsing of temporal distance between the two sanctuaries to assert continuity. lᵉmišmārôt (by guard-stations, by rotations) is the organization principle for all temple service in Chronicles (see 1 Chr 24-26).
24: Gatekeepers at the four compass points: mизrāḥ yāmāh ṣāpôn negeḇ — east, west, north, south. The four-direction scheme places YHWH’s house at the cosmological center. The gatekeeper arrangement mirrors the tribal encampment around the tabernacle (Num 2), where each direction had its assigned tribes — the temple is the wilderness sanctuary translated into permanent architecture.
25: ʾaḥêhem — ‘their fellow Levites’ in villages came to serve šiḇʿat hayyāmîm — ‘every seven days.’ The seven-day rotation for the subsidiary gatekeepers from surrounding villages reflects the same principle as the priestly divisions (24 courses, each serving one week twice per year). The verb yāḇôʾû (Qal imperfect, iterative sense) marks repeated action — this is the ongoing pattern of temple staffing.
26: gᵉḇîrîm — ‘the four chief gatekeepers were entrusted.’ The word gᵉḇîrîm (great men, men of strength) indicates status within the gatekeeper corps. bāṭûaḥ — ‘entrusted’ (Qal passive participle of bāṭaḥ, to trust/rely); the entrusting of the storerooms and treasuries to the chief gatekeepers reflects an institutional trust-arrangement with formal accountability.
27: wayyālînû — ‘they lodged the night around the house of God’ (Qal imperfect, narrative past). The Levitical night-watch at the temple is attested in Psalms 134:1 (‘all you servants of YHWH who stand in the house of YHWH by night’) — a Korahite psalm likely connected to this gatekeeping institution. ûlāhem miftaḥ — ‘to them belonged the opening’ of the temple every morning; the gatekeepers had exclusive custody of the keys and the first access — and the accountability.
28: wᵉqāṣṯ mēhem ʿal kᵉlê hāʿăḇōḏāh — ‘Some of them were over the vessels of service.’ The detailed accounting system — vessels counted in and out (bᵉmispār yᵉḇîʾûm ûbᵉmispār yôṣîʾûm) — reflects the stringent temple accountability recorded in Ezra 1:9-11, where Cyrus’s vessels are inventoried precisely. The Chronicler’s interest in temple vessels as sacred objects requiring careful custody appears repeatedly (1 Chronicles 28:13-14; 2 Chronicles 36:7).
29: The category of those appointed over furnishings and holy vessels (kᵉlê haqqōḏeš) includes oversight of fine flour, wine, oil, incense, and aromatic spices — all the consumable elements of the daily grain offerings, wine libations, and incense. The Levitical management of these commodities reflects a developed temple economy requiring specialized knowledge of ritual quantities and purity standards.
30: wûmin bᵉnê hakkōhᵃnîm — ‘from among the sons of the priests’ some prepared the spice blend (mirsaḥat habbe̊šāmîm). The spice/incense preparation was specifically a priestly — not Levitical — task; Exodus 30:34-38 prescribes the incense formula and prohibits its preparation or use by anyone other than priests for the altar. This verse marks the boundary between Levitical and priestly administrative domains.
31: ʾemûnāh — ‘trusted responsibility’ or ‘faithfulness’ (from ʾāman, to be firm, faithful). Mattithiah’s role over the flat cakes (maḥᵃḇattôt) is described as bᵉʾemûnāh — with faithfulness, in a position of trust. The Chronicler’s emphasis on institutional faithfulness (ʾemûnāh) in temple service reflects the same value that Hezekiah will apply to his treasury oversight (2 Chronicles 31:12,15,18) — the temple economy requires moral as well as ritual fidelity.
32: The Kohathite kinsmen responsible for the rows of bread (leḥem hammaʿărāḵet, ‘showbread,’ literally ‘bread of the arrangement/rows’) — the twelve loaves set out before YHWH each Sabbath (Leviticus 24:5-9). The twice-weekly change of showbread required careful preparation; this verse identifies a specific Kohathite sub-family holding hereditary responsibility for it.
33: hāmᵉšōrᵉrîm rāʾšê ʾāḇôt halᵉwiyyîm — ‘These are the singers, heads of Levitical clans.’ pᵉtûrîm ʿal kol ʾăḇōḏāh — ‘exempt from all other service.’ The word pāṭar (to release, exempt) is relatively rare; the singers’ exemption from the other Levitical administrative duties reflects the Chronicler’s elevation of temple music as primary, not supplementary, worship. They are day-and-night (layl wᵉyômām ʿalêhem bᵉmᵉlāʾḵāh) — the continuous nature of the singers’ service establishes the temple as a place of unceasing worship.
34: The closing summary formula for the Levitical leaders: rāʾšê hāʾāḇôt lalwiyyîm lᵉtôlᵉdôtām rāʾšîm — ‘heads of the Levitical ancestral houses throughout their generations, leaders.’ The phrase yāšᵉḇû bîrûšālāim (‘they lived in Jerusalem’) closes the settler list and serves as a geographical anchor — these Levitical institutions are Jerusalem-based, not provincial.
35: The Saul genealogy (vv35-44) is a near-verbatim repetition of 1 Chronicles 8:29-38, where it was the conclusion of the Benjaminite genealogy. Its reappearance here is a literary technique — the genealogy serves as a runway into the narrative of Saul’s death in 1 Chronicles 10. The founder of Gibeon, yᵉʿîʾēl (Jeiel), is introduced with his wife Maacah; Gibeon will be significant as the site of the tabernacle and high place in David’s era (1 Chronicles 21:29; 2 Chronicles 1:3).
36: The sons of Jeiel/Gibeon: Abdon the firstborn, then five more names including Kish, Ner, and Nadab. The key names in this list are Ner (father of Abner, commander of Saul’s army) and Kish (father of Saul — v39). The genealogy is working toward the explanation of Saul’s lineage from the founder of Gibeon.
37: Four more sons: Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, Mikloth. Ahio appears in 13:7 as one of the men guiding the cart carrying the ark — a member of the Gibeah community that has a role in the subsequent ark narrative. The name connections between genealogy and narrative are characteristic Chronicler technique.
38: Mikloth fathered Shimeam (šimʾām); the note that ‘they also lived in Jerusalem across from their kinsmen’ (wᵉhēm ʾap hēm negeḏ ʾaḥêhem yāšᵉḇû bîrûšālaim ʿim ʾaḥêhem) indicates this Benjaminite branch maintained connection to the Jerusalem community alongside their Gibeah base — a feature that perhaps explains their survival through the Saul catastrophe.
39: wᵉnēr hôlîd ʾet qîš wᵉqîš hôlîd ʾet šāʾûl — the genealogical chain reaches its destination: Ner → Kish → Saul. The Hiphil hôlîd (‘begat/fathered’) is the standard genealogical verb. Saul’s four sons are listed: Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Eshbaal — note that ʾeš bāʿal (‘man of Baal’) is the Chronicler’s form; Samuel uses îš bōšet (‘man of shame,’ a polemical substitution for the Baal element). The Chronicler retains the original theophoric form without the Deuteronomistic bowdlerization.
40: mᵉrîḇ bāʿal — Jonathan’s son is called ‘Meribbaal’ here; Samuel uses ‘Mephibosheth.’ Both the Chronicler’s form (mᵉrîḇ, ‘the one who contends with Baal’ or ‘Baal pleads’) and Samuel’s substitution (mᵉpîḇōšet, ‘from the mouth of shame’) preserve the theophoric element awkwardly — the Chronicler avoids the euphemism, consistent with his retention of Eshbaal in v39.
41: The sons of Micah son of Meribbaal: Pithon, Melech, and Tahrea — three names only; 1 Chronicles 8:35 adds a fourth (‘Ahaz’) while 9:41 has three. This is a textual variant between the two versions of the same genealogy within Chronicles itself, suggesting the source documents had slightly different traditions. The Masoretes supply ‘Ahaz’ in the margin but the BHS text of 9:41 lacks it.
42: The descent from Ahaz: ʾāḥāz hôlîd ʾet yāʿrāh — ‘Ahaz fathered Jarah.’ Note the name yāʿrāh (Jarah) here versus yehôʿaddāh in 1 Chronicles 8:36 — another variant between the two genealogical forms, again pointing to divergent source traditions rather than authorial error. The chain Jarah → Alemeth, Azmaveth, Zimri → Moza continues the Saulide line past the dynasty’s political death.
43: Moza fathered Binea; then rᵉpāyāh (Rephaiah) → ʾelʿāsāh (Eleasah) → āṣēl (Azel). The three-generation chain from Binea to Azel continues the Saulide genealogy six generations past Saul himself — into the post-exilic period, suggesting the Saulide line survived and was tracked by the Chronicler’s sources even though it held no political power after David.
44: Azel’s six sons conclude both the genealogy and chapter 9. The closing formula ʾēlleh bᵉnê āṣēl (‘these are the sons of Azel’) mirrors the chapter’s opening genealogical register. The Saulide line continues beyond the catastrophe narrated in 1 Chronicles 10 — the genealogy provides the frame of survival around the narrative of death. Exactly the same list appears in 1 Chronicles 8:38.
